title: The Architecture of Programmatic Trading
html:
Before you write a single line of trading code, you need a mental map of the whole machine. Automated trading is not one program; it is a pipeline of distinct stages, each of which can fail in its own way. Understanding the boundaries between these stages is what separates a robust bot from a money-shredding accident. This lesson is the architecture you will refer back to for the rest of Part 17.

Not financial advice.
Everything in this part is for education. Code that places orders can lose real money instantly. You will paper-trade everything first, and we will hammer that point repeatedly.

The five stages: data to signal to order to broker to monitor

Every automated strategy, from a one-line moving-average crossover to a hedge-fund execution engine, decomposes into the same five stages:

  1. Data — pull quotes and historical bars from a market-data API (Polygon, Alpha Vantage, yfinance, etc.).
  2. Signal — compute an indicator and decide: buy, sell, or do nothing.
  3. Order — translate the signal into a concrete order (symbol, side, quantity, type, price).
  4. Broker — submit the order through a broker API (Alpaca, IBKR, Tradier) and get a fill back.
  5. Monitor — log everything, watch positions, alert on errors, and enforce kill switches.

The discipline here is to keep these stages loosely coupled. Your signal logic should not know whether the data came from a CSV or a live websocket. Your order logic should not know which broker fills it. When you separate concerns this way, you can backtest the signal stage offline, swap a data provider, or switch from paper to live by changing one variable.

architecture.py
# A skeleton showing the five stages as clean functions. # Each stage takes a simple input and returns a simple output. def fetch_data(symbol): # Stage 1: return a list/frame of historical bars. return get_bars(symbol) def compute_signal(bars): # Stage 2: pure function, no I/O. Easy to test. fast = sma(bars, 10) slow = sma(bars, 30) return 'buy' if fast > slow else 'sell' def build_order(signal, symbol, qty): # Stage 3: signal -> concrete order dict. return {'symbol': symbol, 'side': signal, 'qty': qty, 'type': 'market'} def submit(order): # Stage 4: the only stage that touches the broker. return broker.place(order) def run_once(symbol): bars = fetch_data(symbol) sig = compute_signal(bars) order = build_order(sig, symbol, qty=1) result = submit(order) # <- swap broker for paper here log.info('placed %s', result)

Where does it run?

The second architectural decision is hosting. Your bot needs to run at the right time, reliably, even when your laptop lid is shut. Three broad options:

WhereGood forWatch out for
Your laptop + cronLearning, backtests, manual runsSleeps, wifi drops, you close the lid
Cloud VM (AWS/DigitalOcean)24/7 bots, full controlYou patch the OS; pay hourly
Serverless / scheduled functionRun-once-per-bar jobsCold starts, time limits, statelessness

A common, sane pattern for a beginner: develop on your laptop, then deploy the exact same script to a small cloud VM that wakes on a schedule. We cover hosting in depth in Lesson 17.8.

Synchronous polling vs. event-driven

There are two ways to drive the pipeline. Polling: a scheduler runs your script every N minutes; it fetches the latest bar, computes, maybe orders, and exits. Simple, robust, perfect for end-of-bar strategies. Event-driven: you hold a long-lived websocket connection and react to each tick or trade as it arrives. Lower latency, but far more state to manage and more ways to crash. Beginners should start with polling; almost no retail strategy needs sub-second latency.

Rule of thumb.
If your strategy decides on the close of a bar (most do), you only need to run once per bar. A 15-minute strategy runs four times an hour. You do not need a streaming firehose for that.

The contract between stages

The single most valuable habit is defining the data shape that flows between stages and never breaking it. A bar is {open, high, low, close, volume, timestamp}. A signal is one of buy, sell, flat. An order is a small dict. When each stage honors the contract, you can replace any one piece — backtester for live data, paper broker for real broker — without rewriting the others. That is the entire game.

▸Try It

Sketch the architecture of a strategy you already know.

  1. Pick a simple strategy (e.g., buy when price crosses above its 50-day average).
  2. On paper, write the five stages and what data flows between each.
  3. For each stage, list one way it could fail (bad data, API down, rejected order).
  4. Decide where you would host it and how often it needs to run.

title: Market-Data APIs: Pulling Quotes and Bars in Python
html:
Stage 1 of the pipeline is data. The good news: in 2026 there are several solid market-data APIs with free or cheap tiers, and Python wrappers for all of them. The catch: each has different coverage, rate limits, and quirks. This lesson gives you real, runnable code for the five most common sources and a framework for choosing.

The landscape

ProviderFree tierStrengthLimit
yfinanceYes (unofficial)Zero signup, great for learningUnofficial; can break; not for production
Alpha VantageYes (key)Free historical + fundamentals~25 req/day free; slow
FinnhubYes (key)Quotes, news, decent free tier60 req/min free
Polygon.ioLimited freeBest-in-class bars, tick data5 req/min on free tier
IEX CloudPaidReliable equities dataCredit-based metering
Start here.
For learning and backtests, yfinance needs no account and gets you going in 30 seconds. When you move toward anything live or production, graduate to a paid, keyed provider with an SLA.

yfinance: the zero-friction starting point

yf_demo.py
# pip install yfinance import yfinance as yf # Daily bars for the last 6 months. df = yf.download('AAPL', period='6mo', interval='1d', auto_adjust=True) print(df.tail()) # Open High Low Close Volume # Intraday: 5-minute bars for the last 5 days. intraday = yf.download('MSFT', period='5d', interval='5m') # A quick last price. last = yf.Ticker('SPY').fast_info['last_price'] print('SPY last:', last)

Alpha Vantage: free keyed historical data

alpha_vantage_demo.py
# pip install requests ; free key at alphavantage.co import os, requests KEY = os.environ['ALPHAVANTAGE_KEY'] # never hard-code keys url = 'https://www.alphavantage.co/query' params = { 'function': 'TIME_SERIES_DAILY', 'symbol': 'IBM', 'apikey': KEY, 'outputsize': 'compact', } r = requests.get(url, params=params, timeout=10) data = r.json()['Time Series (Daily)'] for day, bar in list(data.items())[:3]: print(day, bar['4. close'])

Finnhub and Polygon: real-time quotes

quotes_demo.py
import os, requests # Finnhub: real-time quote. pip install finnhub-python optional. fh_key = os.environ['FINNHUB_KEY'] q = requests.get('https://finnhub.io/api/v1/quote', params={'symbol': 'TSLA', 'token': fh_key}).json() print('TSLA current:', q['c'], 'prev close:', q['pc']) # Polygon: aggregate bars (high-quality). 5 req/min on free tier. pg_key = os.environ['POLYGON_KEY'] u = 'https://api.polygon.io/v2/aggs/ticker/AAPL/range/1/day/2026-01-01/2026-06-01' bars = requests.get(u, params={'apiKey': pg_key}).json() print('bars returned:', bars.get('resultsCount'))

Survival rules for data APIs

  • Respect rate limits. Polygon free is 5 req/min. Sleep between calls or you will get HTTP 429.
  • Cache aggressively. Historical bars do not change. Save them to disk and only fetch new bars.
  • Handle gaps and adjustments. Splits and dividends shift historical prices; use adjusted close for backtests.
  • Never trust one source for production. Free data has outages and errors. Cross-check critical numbers.
  • Beware survivorship bias. Many free sources drop delisted tickers, silently inflating backtest results.
Lab note.
Wrap every API call in a small retry-with-backoff helper. A single dropped request should not crash a bot that has been running for three weeks.
▸Try It

Pull and compare data from two sources.

  1. Install yfinance and grab 1 year of daily bars for SPY.
  2. Sign up for a free Alpha Vantage or Finnhub key; store it as an environment variable, not in the file.
  3. Pull the same symbol from the second source and compare the last close.
  4. Note any difference and write down why (adjustments, timing, source).

title: Broker APIs: Alpaca, IBKR, Tradier, Schwab
html:
A data API tells you what the market is doing. A broker API lets you act on it — place orders, check positions, read your account. This lesson surveys the main retail-accessible broker APIs, their authentication models, and the single most important toggle in this entire part: paper vs. live.

Paper first, always.
Every broker below offers a paper (simulated) account. You will build, test, and run for weeks against paper before a single real dollar is at risk. This is not optional caution; it is how professionals work.

The contenders

BrokerAuthPaper?Best for
AlpacaAPI key + secretYes, excellentBeginners; clean REST API
Interactive Brokers (IBKR)TWS/Gateway sessionYesPros; global, every asset class
TradierOAuth tokenYes (sandbox)Options-focused; simple REST
Schwab (ex-TDA)OAuth 2.0LimitedExisting Schwab account holders

Alpaca: the friendliest on-ramp

Alpaca is a commission-free, API-first broker designed for exactly this. Authentication is a key/secret pair, and switching between paper and live is just changing the base URL. That simplicity is why we use it for the worked examples in Lessons 17.4 and 17.7.

alpaca_connect.py
# pip install alpaca-py import os from alpaca.trading.client import TradingClient # paper=True points at the simulated account. NEVER flip this # to False until you have weeks of clean paper runs. client = TradingClient( api_key=os.environ['ALPACA_KEY'], secret_key=os.environ['ALPACA_SECRET'], paper=True, ) acct = client.get_account() print('status:', acct.status, 'cash:', acct.cash, 'buying power:', acct.buying_power)

Interactive Brokers: power, with friction

IBKR is the professional standard — every asset class, global markets, deep liquidity. The cost is complexity: you do not talk to a simple REST endpoint. Instead you run TWS (Trader Workstation) or the headless IB Gateway locally, and your Python connects to it over a socket. The popular ib_insync library makes this manageable.

ibkr_connect.py
# pip install ib_insync ; TWS/Gateway must be running. # Paper port is 7497; live is 7496. Mind the difference! from ib_insync import IB, Stock ib = IB() ib.connect('127.0.0.1', 7497, clientId=1) # 7497 = paper contract = Stock('AAPL', 'SMART', 'USD') ib.qualifyContracts(contract) print(ib.accountSummary()) ib.disconnect()

Tradier and Schwab

Tradier offers a clean REST API with a free sandbox, and is especially popular for options because of its straightforward chains and multi-leg orders. Authentication is a bearer token in the HTTP header. Schwab (which absorbed TD Ameritrade and its well-known API) uses full OAuth 2.0 with a refresh-token dance; it is the option if you already hold a Schwab account, but the onboarding is heavier and approval can take time.

tradier_sandbox.py
import os, requests TOKEN = os.environ['TRADIER_SANDBOX_TOKEN'] base = 'https://sandbox.tradier.com/v1' # sandbox = paper hdr = {'Authorization': f'Bearer {TOKEN}', 'Accept': 'application/json'} r = requests.get(f'{base}/user/profile', headers=hdr, timeout=10) print(r.json())

Rate limits and the tradeoffs that matter

  • Rate limits. Alpaca allows ~200 req/min; IBKR throttles message pacing; Tradier and Schwab publish per-endpoint caps. Build with backoff from day one.
  • Auth lifetime. Keys are long-lived (rotate them); OAuth tokens expire and need refresh logic.
  • Asset coverage. Alpaca = US equities, ETFs, crypto. IBKR = essentially everything. Tradier = US equities and options.
  • Failure modes. A REST broker fails per-request; a session broker (IBKR) fails when the Gateway disconnects — you must detect and reconnect.
The toggle that costs money.
The line between paper and live is one base URL or one boolean. Make it a single, loud, configurable variable in your code — never scatter it. A typo that flips paper to live is a real and common way beginners lose money.
▸Try It

Open and connect to one paper account.

  1. Create a free Alpaca account and generate paper API keys.
  2. Store the key and secret as environment variables.
  3. Run the connect snippet and print your buying power.
  4. Confirm the dashboard shows a simulated balance, not real money.

title: Placing Orders with Alpaca: Market, Limit, Bracket
html:
Now we act. This lesson is a hands-on tour of submitting orders through the Alpaca paper API: market orders, limit orders, and the all-important bracket order that bakes in a stop-loss and take-profit. You will also learn to read your positions and account so the bot knows what it already holds.

Paper account only.
Every snippet here assumes paper=True. Do not change that until you have read the guardrails lesson (17.9) and run clean for weeks.

Order types you actually need

TypeWhat it doesRisk
MarketFills immediately at current priceSlippage on illiquid names
LimitFills only at your price or betterMay never fill
StopBecomes a market order past a triggerGaps blow through the stop
BracketEntry + stop-loss + take-profit togetherBest default for managed risk

A market order

market_order.py
import os from alpaca.trading.client import TradingClient from alpaca.trading.requests import MarketOrderRequest from alpaca.trading.enums import OrderSide, TimeInForce client = TradingClient(os.environ['ALPACA_KEY'], os.environ['ALPACA_SECRET'], paper=True) order = MarketOrderRequest( symbol='AAPL', qty=1, side=OrderSide.BUY, time_in_force=TimeInForce.DAY, ) result = client.submit_order(order) print('order id:', result.id, 'status:', result.status)

A limit order

limit_order.py
from alpaca.trading.requests import LimitOrderRequest # Only buy AAPL if it comes down to 180.00 or lower. limit = LimitOrderRequest( symbol='AAPL', qty=1, side=OrderSide.BUY, limit_price=180.00, time_in_force=TimeInForce.GTC, # good-til-canceled ) result = client.submit_order(limit) print('limit submitted:', result.id)

The bracket order: entry with built-in risk

A bracket order is the single most useful tool for a disciplined bot. It submits your entry and simultaneously arms a stop-loss and a take-profit. The moment your entry fills, both exit legs go live; if one triggers, the other cancels automatically. You can never forget to set a stop again.

bracket_order.py
from alpaca.trading.requests import (MarketOrderRequest, TakeProfitRequest, StopLossRequest) from alpaca.trading.enums import OrderClass # Buy 10 shares, target +5%, hard stop -3%. entry = 190.00 bracket = MarketOrderRequest( symbol='AAPL', qty=10, side=OrderSide.BUY, time_in_force=TimeInForce.DAY, order_class=OrderClass.BRACKET, take_profit=TakeProfitRequest(limit_price=round(entry * 1.05, 2)), stop_loss=StopLossRequest(stop_price=round(entry * 0.97, 2)), ) result = client.submit_order(bracket) print('bracket placed:', result.id)

Reading positions and account state

Before placing anything, a good bot asks: what do I already hold, and what is my buying power? Never assume; always query.

read_state.py
# All open positions. for p in client.get_all_positions(): print(p.symbol, p.qty, 'unrealized P/L:', p.unrealized_pl) # Open (unfilled) orders. from alpaca.trading.requests import GetOrdersRequest from alpaca.trading.enums import QueryOrderStatus open_orders = client.get_orders( GetOrdersRequest(status=QueryOrderStatus.OPEN)) print('open orders:', len(open_orders)) # Cancel everything if you need a clean slate. client.cancel_orders()
Idempotency.
Pass a client_order_id you generate yourself. If your script crashes and reruns, the broker rejects the duplicate id instead of placing the same trade twice.
▸Try It

Place and inspect three order types on paper.

  1. Submit a 1-share market buy and confirm it fills in the dashboard.
  2. Submit a limit buy far below the market and confirm it stays open.
  3. Submit a bracket order and watch the stop and target appear as child orders.
  4. Run the read-state script and reconcile what you see with the dashboard.

title: TradingView Alerts to Webhook to Broker
html:
Not everyone wants to compute signals in Python. Many traders already build their setups visually in TradingView using Pine Script. The bridge from a TradingView alert to a real order is the alert-to-webhook-to-broker pattern: TradingView fires an HTTP POST when your condition triggers, a small server receives it, validates it, and submits an order to your broker. This lesson builds that bridge end to end.

This is powerful and dangerous.
A public webhook that places live trades is a target. Anyone who finds the URL could fire orders. We add a shared secret and run against a paper broker. Treat this lesson as a security exercise as much as a trading one.

The flow

  1. A Pine Script alertcondition (or strategy alert) triggers on TradingView.
  2. The alert sends a JSON message to your webhook URL.
  3. Your receiver verifies a secret, parses the JSON, and builds an order.
  4. The order goes to your paper broker.

Step 1: the Pine alert message

In TradingView, you create an alert and set the message body to JSON. The receiver will parse this. Keep it explicit — symbol, action, quantity, and a secret.

strategy.pine
//@version=5 indicator('MA Cross Alert', overlay=true) fast = ta.sma(close, 10) slow = ta.sma(close, 30) plot(fast), plot(slow) longCond = ta.crossover(fast, slow) shortCond = ta.crossunder(fast, slow) // Use {{strategy.order.action}} in real strategies; here we // hard-code the action per alertcondition. alertcondition(longCond, title='BUY', message='{"secret":"CHANGE_ME","action":"buy","symbol":"AAPL","qty":1}') alertcondition(shortCond, title='SELL', message='{"secret":"CHANGE_ME","action":"sell","symbol":"AAPL","qty":1}')

Step 2: the receiver

A tiny Flask app listens for the POST, checks the secret, and routes to Alpaca paper. Note the validation: we reject anything without the right secret and anything with an unexpected action.

webhook.py
# pip install flask alpaca-py import os from flask import Flask, request, abort from alpaca.trading.client import TradingClient from alpaca.trading.requests import MarketOrderRequest from alpaca.trading.enums import OrderSide, TimeInForce app = Flask(__name__) SECRET = os.environ['WEBHOOK_SECRET'] broker = TradingClient(os.environ['ALPACA_KEY'], os.environ['ALPACA_SECRET'], paper=True) @app.route('/hook', methods=['POST']) def hook(): data = request.get_json(force=True, silent=True) or {} if data.get('secret') != SECRET: abort(403) # reject impostors action = data.get('action') if action not in ('buy', 'sell'): abort(400) side = OrderSide.BUY if action == 'buy' else OrderSide.SELL req = MarketOrderRequest( symbol=data['symbol'], qty=int(data['qty']), side=side, time_in_force=TimeInForce.DAY) o = broker.submit_order(req) return {'ok': True, 'id': str(o.id)} if __name__ == '__main__': app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000)

Step 3: exposing it safely

TradingView must reach your server over the public internet on HTTPS. For testing, a tunneling tool like ngrok gives you a temporary HTTPS URL pointing at localhost:5000. For anything beyond testing, deploy the receiver to a small cloud host with a real domain and TLS.

Webhook security checklist.
Require a long random secret in the body; restrict by source IP where possible; validate every field; cap quantity server-side; rate-limit the endpoint; log every request; and never put broker keys in the alert message — only on the server.

Why a server-side quantity cap matters

The alert says qty:1, but you should never trust the client. A bug in your Pine Script, or a malicious POST, could send qty:100000. Enforce a hard maximum in the receiver and reject anything over it. This is a kill switch hiding in your validation layer.

▸Try It

Wire a TradingView alert to a paper order.

  1. Run webhook.py locally and start an ngrok tunnel to port 5000.
  2. In TradingView, create an alert with the JSON message and your ngrok URL plus /hook.
  3. Trigger the alert (or test-fire it) and confirm a paper order appears in Alpaca.
  4. Add a server-side cap that rejects any qty over 10 and test it with a crafted POST.

title: Backtesting: Backtrader, vectorbt, QuantConnect
html:
Before you risk anything — even paper money over weeks — you want to know how a strategy would have performed historically. That is backtesting. Done well, it builds conviction and exposes flaws. Done badly, it produces beautiful equity curves that evaporate the moment they meet reality. This lesson covers three popular frameworks and, just as importantly, the pitfalls that fool nearly everyone at first.

Three frameworks, three philosophies

ToolStyleBest for
BacktraderEvent-driven, bar-by-barRealistic logic, full control
vectorbtVectorized, NumPy-fastSweeping thousands of parameters
QuantConnectCloud platform (LEAN)Institutional data, live deploy

Backtrader: event-driven and readable

bt_sma.py
# pip install backtrader yfinance import backtrader as bt, yfinance as yf class SmaCross(bt.Strategy): params = dict(fast=10, slow=30) def __init__(self): f = bt.ind.SMA(period=self.p.fast) s = bt.ind.SMA(period=self.p.slow) self.cross = bt.ind.CrossOver(f, s) def next(self): if not self.position and self.cross > 0: self.buy() elif self.position and self.cross < 0: self.close() data = bt.feeds.PandasData(dataname=yf.download('AAPL', period='2y')) cerebro = bt.Cerebro() cerebro.adddata(data) cerebro.addstrategy(SmaCross) cerebro.broker.setcash(10000) cerebro.broker.setcommission(commission=0.001) # model costs! print('start:', cerebro.broker.getvalue()) cerebro.run() print('end:', cerebro.broker.getvalue())

vectorbt: thousands of parameter combos in seconds

vbt_sweep.py
# pip install vectorbt import vectorbt as vbt price = vbt.YFData.download('AAPL', period='2y').get('Close') fast = vbt.MA.run(price, list(range(5, 20)), short_name='fast') slow = vbt.MA.run(price, list(range(20, 60)), short_name='slow') entries = fast.ma_crossed_above(slow) exits = fast.ma_crossed_below(slow) pf = vbt.Portfolio.from_signals(price, entries, exits, fees=0.001) print(pf.total_return().max()) # best combo... be suspicious
That last line is a trap.
Picking the best parameter combo from a giant sweep is overfitting — you found the combination that best fit noise. It will not repeat out of sample. Always validate on data the sweep never saw.

QuantConnect

QuantConnect is a cloud platform built on the open-source LEAN engine. You write strategies in Python in their IDE, backtest against institutional-grade data, and can deploy the same code to live brokers. The advantage is clean, survivorship-bias-free data and a path to production; the tradeoff is working inside their environment and conventions.

The pitfalls that wreck backtests

  • Lookahead bias. Using information not available at decision time (e.g., today's close to trade at today's open). The deadliest and most subtle bug.
  • Survivorship bias. Testing only on companies that still exist; the losers were deleted, inflating returns.
  • Overfitting. Tuning parameters until the curve is gorgeous on history and useless going forward.
  • Ignoring costs. Commissions, spread, and slippage turn many "winners" into losers. Always model fees.
  • Unrealistic fills. Assuming you trade at the exact close, with infinite liquidity, no impact.
The gold standard.
Split your data: optimize on the first portion (in-sample), then test once on the held-out portion (out-of-sample). If it falls apart out of sample, the strategy is fitted noise. Better still, forward-test on paper for weeks before trusting it.
▸Try It

Backtest a crossover and stress-test the result.

  1. Run the Backtrader SMA strategy on 2 years of one ticker.
  2. Re-run it with commission set to 0, then to 0.002, and note how returns change.
  3. Split your data in half; tune the periods on the first half only.
  4. Test those exact periods on the second half. Did the edge survive?

title: Building a Simple End-to-End Automated Bot
html:
Time to assemble the pipeline from Lesson 17.1 into one working program. This bot fetches data, computes an indicator, generates a signal, and submits a paper order — the complete data-to-signal-to-order-to-broker loop in under a hundred lines. We will use a moving-average crossover because it is simple enough to verify by eye, not because it is a good strategy.

Educational, paper only.
A moving-average crossover is a teaching example, not a money-maker. The point is the plumbing: a correct, observable loop you can later drop a real strategy into. Live trading comes only after weeks of clean paper runs and the guardrails in 17.9.

Design principles

  • One decision per bar. Run on a schedule, act on the latest closed bar, then exit.
  • Query before you act. Check current position so you do not double-buy.
  • Log everything. Every run writes what it saw and what it did.
  • Fail safe. On any error, do nothing rather than something wrong.

The bot

bot.py
# pip install alpaca-py yfinance pandas import os, logging import yfinance as yf from alpaca.trading.client import TradingClient from alpaca.trading.requests import MarketOrderRequest from alpaca.trading.enums import OrderSide, TimeInForce logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format='%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(message)s') log = logging.getLogger('bot') SYMBOL = 'AAPL' QTY = 1 FAST, SLOW = 10, 30 client = TradingClient(os.environ['ALPACA_KEY'], os.environ['ALPACA_SECRET'], paper=True) def fetch_signal(): df = yf.download(SYMBOL, period='3mo', interval='1d', auto_adjust=True, progress=False) df['fast'] = df['Close'].rolling(FAST).mean() df['slow'] = df['Close'].rolling(SLOW).mean() last = df.iloc[-1] return 'long' if last['fast'] > last['slow'] else 'flat' def current_qty(): for p in client.get_all_positions(): if p.symbol == SYMBOL: return float(p.qty) return 0.0 def order(side): req = MarketOrderRequest(symbol=SYMBOL, qty=QTY, side=side, time_in_force=TimeInForce.DAY) o = client.submit_order(req) log.info('submitted %s %s -> %s', side, SYMBOL, o.id) def run_once(): try: sig = fetch_signal() held = current_qty() log.info('signal=%s held=%s', sig, held) if sig == 'long' and held == 0: order(OrderSide.BUY) elif sig == 'flat' and held > 0: order(OrderSide.SELL) else: log.info('no action') except Exception as e: log.exception('run failed, doing nothing: %s', e) if __name__ == '__main__': run_once()

How to read this

Trace the five stages: fetch_signal is data + signal, current_qty reads broker state, order is order + broker, and the logging is monitor. The whole thing runs once and exits, which makes it trivial to schedule (next lesson) and impossible to leave hanging in a bad state. The try/except guarantees that any failure logs loudly and changes nothing.

Verify the logic offline first.
Before connecting any broker, run just fetch_signal() and print the signal over several days. Confirm it flips when the lines cross on the chart. Only when the signal is provably correct do you let it touch the order path.

From flat/long to a real position model

This bot only goes flat or long one share. A real version tracks a target position size, reconciles against current holdings, and submits the difference. But the architecture does not change — you are still computing a desired state and nudging the account toward it. Keep that mental model and you can grow this skeleton into anything.

▸Try It

Run the bot against paper and watch it think.

  1. Set your Alpaca paper keys as environment variables.
  2. Run fetch_signal() alone for several symbols and sanity-check the output against charts.
  3. Run the full bot once during market hours and read the log line by line.
  4. Change the symbol to one currently in a downtrend and confirm it stays flat.

title: Operating It: Scheduling, Hosting, Logging, Monitoring
html:
A bot that runs once on your laptop is a toy. A bot you trust with capital runs on schedule, in the cloud, with logs you can read, alerts when it breaks, and graceful handling of the dozens of things that go wrong in production. This lesson is about operations — the unglamorous work that decides whether your automation survives contact with the real world.

Scheduling: when does it run?

Our bot from 17.7 runs once and exits, so we just need something to invoke it on a cadence. On Linux/Mac, cron is the classic tool.

crontab
# Run the bot every weekday at 9:35am US/Eastern, # five minutes after the open. Edit with: crontab -e # min hour dom mon dow command 35 9 * * 1-5 TZ=America/New_York /usr/bin/python3 /home/bot/bot.py >> /home/bot/bot.log 2>&1

If your strategy is intraday, schedule per bar (e.g., */15 for every 15 minutes). For Python-native scheduling, the APScheduler library lets a long-running process trigger jobs without cron.

Hosting: where does it live?

OptionEffortNotes
Cloud VM (DigitalOcean/EC2)MediumFull control; you patch and monitor it
Container (Docker) + hostMediumReproducible env; easy redeploy
Serverless scheduled functionLowGreat for run-once jobs; stateless
Pin your environment.
A backtest that passed last month can break when a library updates. Freeze versions with a requirements.txt and, ideally, a Docker image, so the code that runs live is the exact code you tested.

Logging: your flight recorder

When a trade goes wrong at 2pm, your only witness is the log. Log structured, timestamped lines for every decision, every order, and every error. Write to a file and stdout so both your host and your terminal capture it.

logging_setup.py
import logging from logging.handlers import RotatingFileHandler log = logging.getLogger('bot') log.setLevel(logging.INFO) fmt = logging.Formatter('%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(message)s') # Rotate so logs never fill the disk. fh = RotatingFileHandler('bot.log', maxBytes=1_000_000, backupCount=5) fh.setFormatter(fmt) sh = logging.StreamHandler() sh.setFormatter(fmt) log.addHandler(fh) log.addHandler(sh) log.info('bot started, paper mode')

Monitoring and alerting

Logs are passive; you will not read them until something hurts. Add active alerts for the events that matter: an order rejection, an unhandled exception, or — critically — silence. A bot that stops running is just as dangerous as one that misbehaves.

alert.py
import os, requests def notify(text): # Post to a Slack/Discord incoming webhook. url = os.environ['ALERT_WEBHOOK'] try: requests.post(url, json={'text': text}, timeout=5) except Exception: pass # never let alerting crash the bot # Use it at the edges of run_once: # notify('ORDER REJECTED: ' + str(err)) # notify('bot exception: ' + str(e))
Heartbeat.
Have the bot ping a dead-man's-switch service (or send a daily "still alive" message) on every successful run. If the ping stops, you get alerted that the bot died — the failure mode logs can never tell you about, because a dead bot writes nothing.

Error handling philosophy

  • Retry transient errors (network blips, 429s) with exponential backoff.
  • Fail closed on ambiguity. If you cannot confirm account state, do not trade.
  • Make operations idempotent so a retry never double-fires an order.
  • Alert, then halt on anything you did not anticipate.
▸Try It

Make your bot operable, not just runnable.

  1. Add the rotating-file logging setup and confirm a bot.log appears.
  2. Wire up a Slack or Discord webhook and send yourself a test alert.
  3. Schedule the bot with cron (or APScheduler) to run every weekday.
  4. Add a daily heartbeat message and verify you receive it after a run.

title: Guardrails: Keys, PDT, Limits, Kill Switches, Paper First
html:
This final lesson is the most important in Part 17. Everything before it taught you to make a bot act. This lesson teaches you to make it act safely. Automated trading removes the human pause between idea and execution — which means a bug, a leaked key, or a runaway loop can do damage at machine speed. Guardrails are what stand between your code and a catastrophic, irreversible loss.

Educational, not financial advice.
Nothing here is a recommendation to trade. It is a set of safety practices for people who choose to automate. The first and last rule is: paper-trade first, for a long time.

1. API-key security

Your broker keys are the keys to your money. Treat them like the password to a bank account, because that is what they are.

  • Never hard-code keys in source. They leak into Git history, screenshots, and pastebins.
  • Use environment variables or a secrets manager; load them at runtime.
  • Add .env to .gitignore and double-check before every commit.
  • Scope keys minimally. If your broker offers read-only or trade-only keys, use the least privilege needed.
  • Rotate keys periodically and immediately if you suspect exposure.
secrets.py
# Good: load from environment, fail loudly if missing. import os def require(name): v = os.environ.get(name) if not v: raise RuntimeError(f'missing secret: {name}') return v KEY = require('ALPACA_KEY') SECRET = require('ALPACA_SECRET') # BAD, never do this: # KEY = 'AKABCD1234...' # leaks into git forever

2. The Pattern Day Trader (PDT) rule

In the US, if you make four or more day trades within five business days in a margin account with under $25,000 equity, you are flagged as a Pattern Day Trader and can be restricted from further day trading. A bot can blow through four day trades in minutes without you noticing. Bake awareness of this into your logic: count day trades, and refuse to open a position that would cross the line in a small account.

Know your account type.
PDT applies to US margin accounts. Cash accounts avoid it but introduce settlement constraints (you cannot reuse unsettled funds). Either way, your bot must respect the rules of the account it trades.

3. Position limits and sizing caps

Enforce hard maximums in code, independent of whatever the signal says. The strategy proposes; the guardrails dispose.

limits.py
MAX_POSITION_USD = 1000 # never hold more than this per symbol MAX_ORDER_QTY = 10 # reject any single order above this MAX_OPEN_POSITIONS = 5 def check_order(symbol, qty, price, open_count): if qty > MAX_ORDER_QTY: raise ValueError('order qty exceeds cap') if qty * price > MAX_POSITION_USD: raise ValueError('position size exceeds cap') if open_count >= MAX_OPEN_POSITIONS: raise ValueError('too many open positions') return True

4. The kill switch

Every automated system needs a big red button. A kill switch is a check the bot performs before every order: if a flag file exists, or daily losses exceed a threshold, it stops trading and flattens. You should be able to halt the bot from your phone in seconds.

kill_switch.py
import os MAX_DAILY_LOSS = 200 # dollars; halt if down more than this def trading_halted(account): # Manual switch: create this file to stop instantly. if os.path.exists('HALT'): return True # Automatic switch: daily loss limit. pnl = float(account.equity) - float(account.last_equity) if pnl <= -MAX_DAILY_LOSS: return True return False # Before every order: # if trading_halted(acct): notify('HALTED'); return

5. Why you ALWAYS paper-trade first

Paper trading is not a formality; it is the single most effective guardrail you have. It catches the bugs no backtest can: a wrong sign on a signal, an order that fires twice, a timezone error that trades at the wrong hour, a position-sizing mistake that bets the whole account. None of these cost a cent on paper. All of them cost real money live.

The paper-first discipline.
Run on paper for weeks, across different market conditions. Reconcile every trade against your logs. Only when the bot has behaved exactly as designed — through volatility, through outages, through your own bug fixes — do you consider the smallest possible live allocation. Then you watch it like a hawk.

The guardrail stack, together

  1. Secrets in env vars, never in code.
  2. PDT and account-type checks before opening trades.
  3. Hard position and order caps in code.
  4. A manual and automatic kill switch checked before every order.
  5. Weeks of paper trading before any live dollar.
  6. Monitoring and alerts so a failure wakes you, not your balance.

Build these once, wrap every strategy in them, and you turn automated trading from a way to lose money quickly into a disciplined craft you can practice safely. That is the whole point of this part.

▸Try It

Wrap your bot in a full guardrail stack.

  1. Move all keys to environment variables and confirm nothing sensitive is in your repo.
  2. Add the position-limit checks and try to submit an over-cap order; confirm it is rejected.
  3. Implement the HALT file kill switch and verify creating the file stops trading.
  4. Commit to a written paper-trading plan: how many weeks and what conditions before you would ever consider going live.
